Were You Asked This?
Design Yelp

Design a location-based service like Yelp that allows users to search for nearby businesses using latitude/longitude coordinates, with support for global scale and high query volumes.

Asked at:

Meta

Twilio

OpenAI

Apple

Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

Yelp is a local business discovery and review platform where people search for nearby places (restaurants, salons, mechanics), read ratings and reviews, and share their own experiences with photos. Think of typing "coffee" on your phone and seeing the top spots within a mile, complete with star ratings, hours, and a map.

Interviewers ask this because a Yelp-like system blends geospatial search, full-text ranking, heavy read scaling, and global availability. It tests whether you can constrain a search space efficiently (geohash/quadtree), shard and cache for hotspots (e.g., Times Square), keep a search index consistent with a transactional source of truth, and serve images cost-effectively via CDN. Expect to reason about latency targets (e.g., p95 < 200 ms at 50k QPS), data freshness, and worldwide coverage.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
Yelp

System design answer key for designing a local business review site like Yelp, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to search for nearby businesses by location (lat/long or place name) with optional text and category filters and a chosen radius.

Users should be able to view a business page with name, category, map location, photos, hours, and aggregated ratings with recent reviews.

Users should be able to post one review per business with a 1–5 star rating, optional text and photos, and see the rating impact reflected promptly.

Users should be able to browse on a map (pan/zoom) and see top results in the current viewport with the ability to load more results.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you return 5 relevant places within a 1-mile radius at 50k QPS globally with p95 latency under 200 ms?

How do you shard and rebalance the search/index layer to handle global coverage and hotspots without hot shards?

How will you keep the search index consistent with the source of truth for businesses and reviews at scale?

How do you store, process, and serve business and review photos globally while controlling cost and latency?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Reads

Nearby search is read-heavy with orders of magnitude more reads than writes. Meeting global p95 < 200 ms at 50k QPS requires layered caching (tile/top-K caches), denormalized search documents, minimal payloads for list views, and regional replication to keep data close to users.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Geographic hotspots (downtowns, stadiums, events) create extreme skew that can melt a single shard or cache key. You need adaptive geo sharding, per-key rate limiting, admission control, and precomputed top-K per cell/category to prevent hotspots from dominating tail latency.

Read More
Handling Large Blobs

User-uploaded photos are core to the experience and must be ingested and served efficiently. Using the Large Blobs pattern—object storage, background transformation, and CDN delivery—keeps the core data path fast and cost-effective while providing high cache hit rates globally.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Elasticsearch

Elasticsearch supports geo_point/geo_shape for proximity and polygon queries, full-text ranking for names and categories, and horizontal sharding with routing keys. It’s a strong fit for fast nearby queries across 100M+ locations with facets and relevance scoring built in.

Read More
Redis

Redis delivers microsecond-level access for hot geo tiles, top-5 result caches, and rate limiter counters. It offloads read pressure from the search tier, improves tail latency, and provides simple primitives for per-key throttling in hotspot scenarios.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka enables reliable change data capture from the primary store to the search index and analytics. With partitions keyed by business_id, you can preserve per-entity ordering, build idempotent consumers, and safely retry or dead-letter problematic updates.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Uber

Ride matching also relies on high-QPS geospatial indexing, proximity queries, and hotspot handling in dense urban areas. Techniques like geohash bucketing, regional routing, and aggressive caching carry over directly.

Read
Practice
Tinder

Swipe decks are essentially top-K nearby queries with filters and personalization. Both systems benefit from precomputed results per geo cell, caching, and careful sharding to handle uneven user density.

Read
Practice
FB Post Search

Search over large, frequently updated datasets requires a separate index, CDC pipelines, idempotent upserts, and strategies for reindexing. The consistency and latency trade-offs are very similar to a Yelp-like search tier.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Naively scanning lat/long ranges without a geo index

B-tree range scans over latitude/longitude don’t scale to 100M locations and will time out in dense areas. Without geohash/quadtree indexing and precise distance filtering, you won’t meet p95 < 200 ms or control tail latency.

Treating the search cluster as the source of truth

Elasticsearch is not transactional; writes can be lost or reordered during failures. Without CDC, idempotent upserts, and versioning, your index will drift from the primary database, causing stale or incorrect results.

Ignoring hotspots and caching for nearby queries

Sending all traffic directly to the index at 50k QPS—especially for popular city centers—creates hot shards and poor tail latency. You need layered caching (tile/top-K), adaptive sharding, and per-key rate limiting to stay within SLAs.
